entries.extra: confuse with: Plural: formulae or formulas
confuse with: Stress: noun vs verb

entries.meaning: a plan to achieve a goal

entries.extra: confuse with: Stress: noun vs verb

entries.example: There is a dearth of affordable housing.
There are a plethora of options for online learning.
The plan succeeded, albeit with delays.

entries.meaning: claimed to be true but not proven
entries.example: He allegedly sold the data to a competitor.
The minister alluded to upcoming reforms.
The public is ambivalent about the new tax.

entries.meaning: to increase the strength or effect of
entries.example: Social media amplifies public opinion quickly.
This is arguably the most important factor.
Growth came at the expense of the environment.

entries.meaning: to weaken or reduce in force
entries.example: Trees attenuate noise from the motorway.
She qualified by virtue of her experience.
New data calls into question earlier conclusions.
The merger will consolidate the company's market share.
Critics contend that the policy is unfair.
Balancing growth and sustainability is a conundrum.

entries.meaning: on the other hand / in contrast
entries.example: Cities are growing; conversely, rural areas are shrinking.
Independent data corroborates the original findings.
The report delineates three policy options.

entries.meaning: a sharp division between two opposites
entries.example: There is a false dichotomy between work and rest.
There was a slight dip in attendance in July.

entries.meaning: a difference between two things that should match
entries.example: There is a discrepancy between reported and actual figures.
Income disparity has widened over the decade.
The author elucidates a complex theory in plain terms.
Ecosystems depend on a delicate equilibrium.

entries.meaning: open to more than one interpretation
entries.example: The results were equivocal and required further study.
Traffic congestion is exacerbated by poor planning.

entries.meaning: to be a typical example of
entries.example: This case exemplifies the wider problem.
It is risky to extrapolate from such a small sample.
Rules exist for the sake of safety.
She is a formidable opponent in debate.

entries.meaning: until now / up to this time
entries.example: The cause of the disease was hitherto unknown.
The findings implicate stress as a major factor.
Employees took extra leave in lieu of a bonus.
This year's results stand in stark contrast to last year's.
Computers are indispensable in modern offices.

entries.meaning: to conclude from evidence rather than direct statement
entries.example: We can infer from the graph that demand is falling.
The signal is intermittent in remote areas.

entries.meaning: to place side by side for comparison
entries.example: The essay juxtaposes urban and rural lifestyles.
There was a marked increase in cycling commuters.
The data was collected with meticulous attention to detail.
Vaccines mitigate the spread of disease.
Notwithstanding the cost, the project went ahead.
The species is on the brink of extinction.
She left, ostensibly to take a phone call.
Patient safety is of paramount importance.
Stereotypes are perpetuated by media coverage.
Stock prices plummeted after the announcement.

entries.meaning: to put forward as a fact or basis for argument
entries.example: Scientists posit that the climate has shifted abruptly.
Refugees often face a desperate predicament.
A degree is a prerequisite for the role.
The delay was presumably caused by weather.
We presume the data is accurate unless proven otherwise.
The prevalence of diabetes is rising globally.
It is prudent to save for emergencies.
The ruling has serious legal ramifications.

entries.meaning: an indirect effect of an event
entries.example: Job losses have wide social repercussions.
Water scarcity affects millions in dry regions.
She is scrupulous about citing sources.
The project is seemingly on track.

entries.meaning: to rise quickly to a high level

entries.example: Rainfall has been sporadic this season.
The graph shows a steep decline in print sales.

entries.meaning: to support a claim with evidence
entries.example: The study substantiates the link between diet and longevity.
Her answer was succinct yet complete.
Cut any superfluous detail from your essay.
There was a surge in online shopping during 2020.

entries.meaning: as a result / by that means
entries.example: She missed the deadline, thereby losing the contract.
Income above this threshold is taxed at a higher rate.
He overworked, to the detriment of his health.

entries.meaning: the lowest point on a graph
entries.example: Sales reached a trough in February.

entries.meaning: to support / form the basis of

entries.example: The report underscores the urgency of action.
There is unequivocal evidence of warming.
Avoid verbose answers in Speaking Part 1.
A scheme whereby graduates can defer fees was introduced.

entries.meaning: reach a decision based on evidence

entries.example: Admission is free for children under 5.
Time was allocated for group work.
At high altitudes, oxygen is thin.
We need to analyse the data.
The annual festival is in July.
I'd like to make an appointment for Tuesday.
The architect designed the new library.
The final assessment is an essay.
The castle is a popular tourist attraction.
The local authorities approved the plan.
Is this room available on the 15th?
The balance is due on arrival.
Include a bibliography at the end.
There's a biography of the founder.
I'll send you a brochure about our tours.
The recycling campaign starts next week.
The hall has a capacity of 500.
Carbon emissions are a major concern.
You'll receive a certificate on completion.
You can pay by cheque or card.
The bridge collapsed in the storm.
The local community organized the event.
We can conclude the method works.
The city council funded the project.
The curriculum includes theory and practice.
The deadline for registration is March 1st.
Clear the debris from the site.
I'll demonstrate how to use the machine.
Our final destination is the museum.
We need to devise a new strategy.
Dinosaurs lived millions of years ago.
There's a display of local artwork.
Her dissertation was on climate change.

entries.meaning: a gift of money or goods

entries.example: Submit your first draft by Friday.
Duration of the course is 12 weeks.
Students are eligible for a discount.
I'm calling to make an enquiry.
Soil erosion is a serious problem.
Can I get an extension on the deadline?
The facilities include a gym and pool.
The findings suggest a new trend.
Meet at the fountain in the square.
She works in the hospitality industry.
How do you interpret these results?
Here's the itinerary for the trip.
The tower is a well-known landmark.
The leisure centre has a pool.
You can store your luggage here.
The building is closed for maintenance.
The municipal government funded the park.
The main objective is to test the theory.

entries.meaning: land surrounded by water on 3 sides

entries.extra: trap: Stress changes: noun vs verb

entries.example: The village is on a plateau.
Smoking is not allowed on the premises.
Explain the rationale for your choice.
The park is used for recreation.
You can get a full refund.
A bronze sculpture in the garden.

entries.meaning: a plan to achieve a goal

entries.example: They live in the northern suburbs.
There's a supplement for a sea view.
Can I have your surname, please?
The terrain is rocky and dry.

entries.meaning: to move from one place to another

entries.example: The goods are kept in a warehouse.

entries.word: According to the research / The data shows
entries.meaning: Factual — this is usually the answer
First answer is WRONG. Real answer follows.
entries.example: It's on Monday... actually, I meant Tuesday.

entries.meaning: The OPPOSITE of what you expect is coming

entries.example: I initially thought it was hard, but it was manageable.

entries.word: I see what you mean, however...
I thought it was X, but it's actually Y
entries.meaning: Y is the answer, not X.
entries.example: I thought it was in Building A, but it's actually Building C.

entries.meaning: Full agreement — both speakers share this view

entries.word: In my opinion / I think / I believe / I feel
entries.meaning: Personal view — may differ from facts
What follows 'instead of' is NOT the answer.
entries.example: Instead of Wednesday, let's meet on Thursday.
It used to be free, but now there's a £5 charge.

entries.word: It's widely believed that / Many assume
entries.meaning: Common belief — speaker may disagree next

entries.example: Let me correct that — the deadline is Friday, not Thursday.
The price is $15... no, wait, it's $50.
Oh, I've just been told the venue has changed.
On second thought, let's go with Plan B.

entries.word: Originally it was X, but now it's Y
entries.example: Originally it was scheduled for June, but now it's July.

entries.word: Rather than X, I'd suggest Y
entries.example: Rather than the bus, I'd suggest the train.

entries.word: Some people say / It's often thought
entries.meaning: General opinion — may be contradicted next

entries.word: Sorry, I was wrong about that...
entries.example: Sorry, I was wrong — it's the 3rd floor, not the 2nd.

entries.meaning: Partial agreement — the real view comes after 'but'

entries.word: That's exactly what I was thinking
Well, I was going to say X, but...
entries.meaning: X is rejected. Answer comes after 'but'.
entries.example: I was going to suggest the library, but the lab is better.

entries.word: What I should have said was...
entries.example: What I should have said was the morning session.

entries.meaning: person in family from long ago

entries.example: She has an aptitude for maths.

entries.meaning: to use machines to do work

entries.example: The oil spill had catastrophic effects.
A compulsion to check the door.
Millions of records in the database.
Seeds are dispersed by the wind.
The efficacy of the vaccine is high.
The country is in a recession.

entries.word: Articles (a, an, the) count as words
Hyphenated words count as ONE word (e.g. well-known = 1 word)
If it says TWO WORDS, writing THREE = zero marks even if correct
Numbers can be written as words or digits (7 or seven)
These headings may match details but NOT the main idea — eliminate them

entries.meaning: Qualified statements match qualified passage language. 'Some researchers believe...' in passage + 'Some experts think...' in question = TRUE.
entries.extra: Qualifying Words — Often make statements TRUE

entries.meaning: If the passage says 'most' but the question says 'all', it's likely FALSE. Absolute words rarely match qualified statements.
entries.extra: Absolute Words — Often make statements FALSE

entries.meaning: If the passage says 'most' but the question says 'all', it's likely FALSE. Absolute words rarely match qualified statements.
entries.extra: Absolute Words — Often make statements FALSE

entries.meaning: If the passage says 'most' but the question says 'all', it's likely FALSE. Absolute words rarely match qualified statements.
entries.extra: Absolute Words — Often make statements FALSE

entries.meaning: If the passage says 'A causes B' but the question says 'B causes A', it's FALSE — the direction is reversed.
If the passage says 'more effective than X' but the question says 'as effective as X', it's FALSE.
If the passage says 'was once common' but the question says 'is common', it's FALSE.
entries.extra: Time Words — Past vs Present traps
